cards.text: Three different investigations point at three different things about Chinese-owned platforms. Bundling them produces a story stronger than the evidence.
cards.sub: Citizen Lab on WeChat file surveillance (2020), Sinopsis on one pro-Russia narrative reaching Chinese-language Malaysian audiences (2024), ISEAS on RedNote's PRC content rules (2025). The shared thread is platform opacity — not what readers believe.

cards.h: WeChat surveils images and documents shared between non-China-registered accounts to train its China-side political censorship system
cards.s: Citizen Lab at Toronto documented the file-surveillance mechanism in 2020. The same paper is explicit it could not test ordinary chat-text between non-China accounts and found no evidence linking the surveillance to CCP direction.
cards.d: Tencent's WeChat performs server-side keyword filtering on messages involving China-registered accounts and surveils images and documents shared between users registered outside China to expand the database that censors China-registered users. Citizen Lab's May 2020 paper 'We Chat, They Watch' is the most-cited technical evidence. The same paper is explicit on two limits routinely lost in secondary commentary: the methodology could not test whether ordinary chat-text between non-China accounts was surveilled, and Citizen Lab explicitly stated it had no evidence attributing this surveillance behaviour to direction from the Chinese government. ISEAS Perspective 2025/99 separately documents that Xiaohongshu (RedNote) operates a single global version under PRC content rules and references a 2022 leaked internal censorship document covering categories including CCP and government criticism. ISEAS reports that ethnic Chinese users are over-represented in RedNote's Malaysian user base relative to their roughly 23% share of the general population (DOSM 2024).

cards.h: Malaysia licensed WeChat and TikTok in January 2025; broadened to a deemed-registration regime in January 2026; the Online Safety Act 2025 explicitly excludes private messaging from its scope
cards.s: The licences regulate platform presence; the Act regulates harmful content but not in private messages. Neither requires platforms to disclose moderation algorithms or recommender weights to MCMC.
cards.d: On 1 January 2025 Malaysia's Applications Service Provider Class licence regime came into effect, requiring platforms with at least eight million Malaysian users to hold a licence. Tencent's WeChat and ByteDance's TikTok were the first two platforms to obtain it. From 1 January 2026 MCMC moved to a deemed-registration approach (Declaration No. 87 of 2025 issued 15 December 2025) covering the wider major-platform set. The Online Safety Act 2025 entered into force in the same window, but Section 2 of the Act explicitly excludes 'a private messaging feature of any applications service' from its scope — directly relevant because WeChat's documented file-surveillance behaviour is a private-messaging matter. At the January 2025 licensing launch, the penalty for unlicensed operation under Communications and Multimedia Act Section 126 was up to RM500,000 or up to five years' imprisonment; CMA amendments came into force in February 2026 and the consolidated penalty levels should be re-verified before any reader publication. MCMC's April 2026 disclosure of 463 fake-news takedown requests broke down by platform (Facebook 185, TikTok 142, Threads 131) but the public report did not disaggregate by language of content. No public licensing instrument located in this verification requires disclosure of keyword filters, recommender weights or category-based moderation rules to MCMC.

cards.h: Chinese-Malaysian favourability of China rose from 41% to 67% (2016 to 2022); Malay favourability declined 33% to 28% in the same window. The divergent ethnic trajectories rule out a single-platform explanation
cards.s: Reported by RFA citing the joint Merdeka Center and UM China Studies Institute survey, with the same figures reproduced in Oriental Daily. Documented Malay-language disinformation on TikTok and Facebook around the November 2022 election complicates any Chinese-platform-only framing.
cards.d: Merdeka Center and Universiti Malaya China Studies Institute jointly surveyed Malaysian views of China in 2016 and 2022. The ethnic-Chinese cross-tab favourability rose 41% to 67%; the ethnic-Malay cross-tab declined 33% to 28%; Indian-Malaysian respondents had the highest unfavourable share. These figures reach this analysis through Asia Fact Check Lab / RFA reporting (July 2023) and Oriental Daily reporting (May 2022) — a Malaysian Chinese-language primary source the original analysis was missing. Until the underlying Merdeka / UM cross-tab dataset is obtained, the figures should be cited as 'as reported by'. Two implications follow. First, the divergent ethnic trajectories rule out any simple causal story where one platform changed minds across the board — they did not. Second, Malaysian closed-platform problems are not Chinese-language-only. UKM and ANU researchers Hew Wai Weng and Nicholas Chan documented systematic Malay-language disinformation on TikTok and Facebook around the November 2022 general election; in the same period MCMC and police investigated paid TikTok campaigns spreading May 13 racial-riot imagery. The ISEAS State of Southeast Asia 2024 survey separately reported Malaysia's preference for ASEAN alignment with China rose 20.3 percentage points year-on-year, but that is a regional elite/opinion-leader survey, not a nationally representative mass-public poll.

cards.text: The question is not which Malaysian community believes Beijing. It is whether any Malaysian — Chinese-, Malay-, Tamil-, English-language — gets to see how their platform decides what news reaches them.

cards.h: Think of it like a landlord who decides which tenants' mail gets delivered
cards.text: The story is not what the tenants believe. It is that the landlord writes the rules, the rules are not public, and Malaysia's regulator licenses the landlord without seeing the rules. WeChat, TikTok, RedNote, Facebook and Telegram are all landlords; Malaysians of every community are the tenants.

Three different investigations point at three different things — Citizen Lab in 2020 on WeChat file surveillance, Sinopsis in 2024 on a single pro-Russia narrative reaching Chinese-language Malaysian audiences, ISEAS in 2025 on RedNote's globally-uniform PRC content rules — and bundling them as one story overstates what is jointly proven. Two structural facts are nonetheless harder to dispute. First, PRC-headquartered platforms operate under the National Intelligence Law of 2017, whose Article 7 sets a general duty on organisations to support and assist national intelligence work — a legal exposure that does not bind US- or EU-headquartered platforms in the same way. Second, Malaysia's 2025–2026 platform regime regulates presence and harmful content, but the Online Safety Act 2025 explicitly excludes private messaging from its scope and no licensing instrument located requires disclosure of moderation algorithms to MCMC. Transparency-of-moderation requirements that would close the WeChat-and-RedNote gap would also expose the Malay-language disinformation networks UKM and ANU researchers documented around the 2022 election, and would give regulators and victims of gendered or scam harms — across every language — a way to test whether reports are being processed. Naming the jurisdictional asymmetry honestly while applying the standard universally is the only frame that survives both halves of the evidence. The case for transparency does not require asserting that any community is being deceived — it rests on the simpler observation that platforms now reaching most of the country should be auditable to it.

headline: Three Investigations, One Platform-Transparency Gap — and a Survey Number Bigger Than Most Think
context: WeChat, TikTok and RedNote operate under PRC content rules and reach a substantial share of Malaysian users. Malaysia licensed the first two in January 2025 and broadened to a deemed-registration regime in January 2026, but the Online Safety Act 2025 explicitly excludes private messaging and no licensing instrument requires platforms to disclose moderation algorithms to MCMC. The Merdeka Center / UM survey showed Malaysian-Chinese favourability of China rose from 41% to 67% over 2016–2022, while Malay favourability declined 33% to 28% — divergent ethnic trajectories that rule out a single-platform causal story.
sources: Citizen Lab (University of Toronto) 'We Chat, They Watch' May 2020; ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute Perspectives 2022/41 (Loh & Mustaffa), 2022/90 (Wang Zheng), 2024/46 (Lee & Choong), 2025/99 (Wang Zheng) and State of Southeast Asia 2024 Survey Report; Freedom House 'Beijing's Global Media Influence 2022 — Malaysia'; Sinopsis (Prague) Sep 2024; Asia Fact Check Lab / Radio Free Asia Jul 2023; Malaysiakini Hong Kong infowar investigation 2019; Merdeka Center + Universiti Malaya China Studies Institute joint survey via RFA and Oriental Daily 2022; SoyaCincau / PYMNTS / Reuters citing MCMC 1 Jan 2025; New Straits Times / Bernama Apr 2026; Federal Gazette P.U.(A) 206/2024; MCMC Declaration No. 87 of 2025 (15 Dec 2025); Online Safety Act 2025 (Act 866); China Law Translate / National Intelligence Law of the PRC 2017 Article 7; Stratsea (Hew Wai Weng & Nicholas Chan 2023); Malay Mail TikTok/May 13 investigation 30 Nov 2022; DOSM Demographic Statistics Q3 2024.
confidence: Medium-High. Platform-architecture claims (Citizen Lab on WeChat, ISEAS on RedNote, MCMC ASP licensing regime, MCMC Declaration 87/2025, Online Safety Act 2025 s.2 private-messaging exclusion, PRC National Intelligence Law Art. 7) are documented by primary technical, regulatory and legal sources. Survey claims (41%→67% Chinese; 33%→28% Malay; +20.3pp ISEAS) are reported but the underlying Merdeka cross-tab dataset is not openly published — the citation chain runs through RFA and Oriental Daily. Specific commercial figures (~12M WeChat users; ~65–70% RedNote ethnic-Chinese share; '10 systematically censored RedNote categories') were dropped from cards because Stage 2 and Stage 3 could not verify them from primary sources. The CMA Section 126 penalty figure carries a stale-risk caveat after February 2026 amendments. The brief flagged HOLD pending two prerequisites — at least two Chinese-language Malaysian primary voices and a parallel brief on Malay-language closed-platform narratives. The first is partially addressed by the Oriental Daily citation introduced here; the second remains open. This synthesis is generated for analysis purposes, not publication.
revisions_made: REPHRASED (Stage 2 bias / MISLEADING_FRAMING): 'The platform timeline rules out the simplest causal story' → 'The divergent ethnic trajectories rule out a single-platform explanation'. Stage 2 correctly flagged that conflating licensing-date with adoption-date was a logical leap; the corrected framing rests the causal-rejection on the divergent ethnic data, not on the licensing regime.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 / OVERCLAIM): 'WeChat scans files shared between non-China-registered accounts...' tightened to 'WeChat surveils images and documents shared between non-China-registered accounts' and qualified with 'methodology could not test whether ordinary chat-text between non-China accounts was surveilled'. Stage 3 verified that Citizen Lab tested files (images/documents) but explicitly could not test chat-text.

CORRECTED (Stage 2 + Stage 3 / UNVERIFIED_DETAIL): 'leaked 2022 internal document listing ten systematically censored categories' → 'a 2022 leaked internal censorship document covering categories including CCP and government criticism'. The specific number 'ten' could not be verified by either reviewer; the leak's existence is documented.

CORRECTED (Stage 2 + Stage 3 / UNVERIFIED_DETAIL): 'Approximately 65–70% of Malaysian RedNote users identify as ethnic Chinese, compared with about 23% of the general population' → 'ISEAS reports that ethnic Chinese users are over-represented in RedNote's Malaysian user base relative to their roughly 23% share of the general population (DOSM 2024)'. The 65–70% figure could not be verified by either Stage 2 or Stage 3; the directional claim is preserved with the DOSM denominator separately verified.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 / OVERCLAIM): 'Three independent investigations document that closed Chinese-owned platforms apply content rules written under PRC jurisdiction to users worldwide' → 'Three different investigations point at three different things — Citizen Lab on WeChat file surveillance, Sinopsis on a single pro-Russia narrative, ISEAS on RedNote content rules — and bundling them overstates what is jointly proven'. Stage 3 correctly flagged the original synthesis as merging unlike evidence; Sinopsis documents a propaganda narrative, not platform architecture.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 / MISLEADING_FRAMING): 'Penalty for unlicensed operation is up to RM500,000 or imprisonment up to five years' qualified with 'At the January 2025 licensing launch' and 'CMA amendments came into force in February 2026 and the consolidated penalty levels should be re-verified'. Stage 3 flagged stale risk after the Feb 2026 amendments.

CORRECTED (Stage 3 / UNVERIFIED): 'Malaysian regulators do not see the keyword filters, recommendation weights or category-based moderation rules' → 'No public licensing instrument located in this verification requires disclosure of keyword filters, recommender weights or category-based moderation rules to MCMC'. Stage 3 correctly noted the negative claim was over-strong; the softer phrasing reflects what the search actually established.
CORRECTED (Stage 3 / MISLEADING): 'This is the strongest Malaysian primary-source signal' dropped — Stage 3 noted it overstates the source chain (figures reach the analysis through RFA, not direct).

CORRECTED (Stage 3 / UNVERIFIED): 'Gaza/Israel identified by ISEAS analysts as a major driver' framing dropped from cards — Stage 3 could not verify the exact 'major driver across Muslim-majority ASEAN states' formulation.

ADDED (Stage 4 ChatGPT): Online Safety Act 2025 Section 2 private-messaging exclusion added to Political fact card and view. Verified primary source: lom.agc.gov.my Act 866. This is the most consequential gap in the original Stage 1 — directly relevant to WeChat closed messaging.

ADDED (Stage 4 ChatGPT): MCMC Declaration No. 87 of 2025 (deemed-registration regime, effective 1 Jan 2026) added to Political fact card. Updates the framing for the current period and addresses Stage 3's stale-risk flag on '...the only platforms licensed'.

ADDED (Stage 4 DeepSeek): Malay favourability decline 33% → 28% (2016 → 2022) added to Social fact card. Verified primary source: Oriental Daily 25 May 2022 — a Malaysian Chinese-language primary source that partially closes the brief's flagged source-spectrum gap. This is the single most important addition to the Social card; it converts an under-evidenced 'platform timeline rules out causation' argument into a properly evidenced 'divergent ethnic trajectories rule out a single-platform explanation' argument.

ADDED (Stage 4 DeepSeek): UKM/ANU researchers Hew Wai Weng and Nicholas Chan on Malay-language TikTok/Facebook disinformation around GE15 (Stratsea 2023, Malay Mail 2023). Added to Social fact card and View. This addition is what makes the universalist transparency frame defensible — without it, the analysis reads as Chinese-language-platform-only.

ADDED (Stage 4 DeepSeek): MCMC and police investigation of paid TikTok campaigns spreading May 13 racial-riot imagery during GE15 (Malay Mail 30 Nov 2022). Added to Social card. Concrete documented case that platform-opacity problems are not Chinese-language-only.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5 / DIPLOMATIC_COWARDICE): View card now explicitly names PRC National Intelligence Law 2017 Article 7. Verified primary source: chinalawtranslate.com translation of the Law. Stage 5 correctly flagged this as the load-bearing legal asymmetry the analysis was avoiding. Naming it does not require any ethnic-character claim — the asymmetry is about the platforms' jurisdictional exposure, not about who uses them.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5 / FALSE_BALANCE): The view card no longer claims that 'the same standard should apply to Malay- and English-language platforms; treating only Chinese-language ones as the problem would be the more dangerous mistake' as a flat symmetry. Replaced with 'Naming the jurisdictional asymmetry honestly while applying the standard universally is the only frame that survives both halves of the evidence' — preserves the universalist transparency standard while no longer denying the PRC-jurisdiction asymmetry.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): 'DOSM 2024' as denominator citation for the ~23% Chinese share of population. Verified against DOSM Demographic Statistics Third Quarter 2024. This was needed because the 65–70% RedNote figure was dropped; the denominator side of the comparison is still defensible.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): Reframe revised from 'what transparency any Malaysian user gets on how their platform moderates news' to 'whether any Malaysian — Chinese-, Malay-, Tamil-, English-language — gets to see how their platform decides what news reaches them'. Self-verified: this is a question-restructuring reframe per CLAUDE.md rules; the language-list is descriptive not factual; no primary source needed.

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): Analogy now lists 'WeChat, TikTok, RedNote, Facebook and Telegram' rather than only 'WeChat, TikTok and RedNote'. The expanded list is supported by Stage 4 ChatGPT's MCMC Declaration No. 87 reference (which covers WhatsApp, Telegram, Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube as within the framework).

introduced_claims.claim: DOSM 2024 puts Chinese share of Malaysia's general population at roughly 23%
introduced_claims.primary_source: DOSM Demographic Statistics Third Quarter 2024 (released 13 Nov 2024) — verified by Stage 3 in its 'omitted_facts' citation

introduced_claims.claim: Naming PRC National Intelligence Law 2017 Article 7 in the view card
introduced_claims.primary_source: chinalawtranslate.com translation of Law of the People's Republic of China on National Intelligence (2017), Article 7: 'All organizations and citizens shall, in accordance with the law, support, assist, and cooperate with national intelligence work...' — verified by Stage 5

synthesis_notes:
Six material over-integration decisions, made deliberately rather than averaged:

(1) Stage 4 ChatGPT (broaden to all-platform governance) vs Stage 5 (name the PRC jurisdictional asymmetry) point in apparently opposite directions but are NOT contradictory — the synthesis honours both. The view card names the National Intelligence Law asymmetry (Stage 5's load-bearing point) AND applies the transparency standard universally because Malay-language TikTok disinformation is also documented (Stage 4 DeepSeek's load-bearing addition). This is additive integration, not averaging.

(2) Stage 5 wanted explicit naming of 'the ethnic segmentation of information ecosystems' and a 'linguistically and culturally parallel information ecosystem that diverges from Malay- and English-dominant spheres'. REJECTED per CLAUDE.md 3R rule — the analysis must critique platforms, not communities. The 41%→67% Chinese / 33%→28% Malay divergence is verified data and is stated as data; characterising the community itself as 'segmented from the polity' is the line the analysis declines to cross.

(3) Stage 5 cited a '90% in 2024 Merdeka poll' figure for Chinese-Malaysian favourability. DROPPED per the stage 6 preamble: I could not independently verify the specific 90% Chinese cross-tab from the December 2024 Merdeka poll within reasonable effort. The 2022 figures (41%→67%) carry the social card; introducing an unverified 2024 number would commit the same UNVERIFIED_DETAIL sin Stage 3 was correcting.

(4) Stage 4 ChatGPT and Stage 4 DeepSeek both flagged a gender gap; Stage 5 explicitly disagreed, saying gender is not the load-bearing dimension here. Decision: Stage 5 is right that gender is not the central frame, but the view card incorporates the Stage 4 point briefly ('would give regulators and victims of gendered or scam harms — across every language — a way to test whether reports are being processed') without adding a separate fact card. Not averaging — honouring Stage 5's framing primacy while preserving Stage 4's substantive concern.

(5) Stage 4 DeepSeek's counter-argument that the 'landlord' analogy 'conflates private content moderation with state censorship' (citing Citizen Lab's no-CCP-direction caveat). PARTIALLY ACCEPTED: the analogy is preserved because it captures the opacity-and-licensing-without-audit point, but the Technology fact card now explicitly preserves Citizen Lab's no-CCP-direction qualifier so the analogy cannot be read as alleging state direction.

(6) Compression decision: the Social fact card now carries the 33%→28% Malay decline alongside the 41%→67% Chinese rise. This nearly doubles the card's character count but is load-bearing — without the Malay decline, the 'rules out single-platform explanation' argument has no evidentiary basis and reduces to the original Stage 2-flagged misleading framing. The qualifier 'as reported by RFA citing the joint Merdeka / UM survey, with the same figures reproduced in Oriental Daily' is preserved because dropping the citation chain would convert two reported figures into asserted facts.

Separately: Stage 4 ChatGPT proposed substantial international parallels (EU DSA Art. 34, Singapore IMDA, India IT Rules) and Stage 4 DeepSeek added Indonesia. None made it into the cards because the bite-size budget cannot carry them; they belong in a follow-up issue on Malaysia's regulatory choices rather than padded into this one. They are recorded in the brief's open questions for future treatment.
stage_scores_notes: ba derived as 100 minus Stage 2 bias_score=15 (low bias detected). fc is Stage 3 factual_accuracy_score=68 directly. af averaged across Stage 4 ChatGPT (74) and Stage 4 DeepSeek (65) = 69.5, rounded to 70. ct is Stage 5 courage_score=38 directly. sr (synthesis review) self-assessed at 84 because every Stage 2/3 cardinal-sin flag was either applied or explicitly addressed in revisions_made; every Stage 4 verified addition was integrated; Stage 5's load-bearing flag (PRC National Intelligence Law) was integrated; the over-integration decisions are documented in synthesis_notes. Score reduced from a potential 90 because the brief's spectrum gap is only partially closed (one Chinese-language Malaysian source added; the parallel Malay-language brief is not done) and the Merdeka primary cross-tab dataset is still not directly accessed.
final_score_method: Equal-weight average across all six stage scores (pa+ba+fc+af+ct+sr)/6 = (80+85+68+70+38+84)/6 = 70.83. Note this differs from the standard 4-stage final ((pa+ba+fc+sr)/4 = 79.25) because the user explicitly insisted Stages 4 and 5 be factored into the synthesis despite their retired status in the current playbook.
